title: LÍF659M-shell-intro
text:
Preface
This workshop is part of LÍF659M at the University of Iceland. It introduces the Unix shell — the command-line interface that underpins most bioinformatics work.
The material is adapted from the Data Carpentry: Shell Genomics lesson and Clara Jégousse’s previous shell workshops.

title: LÍF659M-shell-intro
text:
Learning objectives
By the end of this session you will be able to:

Open a terminal and navigate your file system using the command line
Create, copy, move and inspect files and directories
Install a command-line tool
Download a biological sequence file from NCBI
Perform basic exploration of a FASTA file using grep, wc and pipes

title: LÍF659M-shell-intro
text:
Schedule








Time
Chapter
Topic




00:00

Opening a terminal on your OS


00:10

What is the shell and why use it?


00:25

Moving around directories


00:50

Creating, copying and inspecting files

which, package managers, Entrez Direct

Key points and next steps

title: LÍF659M-shell-intro
text:
Setup
Please complete the setup steps before the session — this means only opening your terminal. Everything else will be installed during class.

MacOS
The Terminal application is already on your Mac. Open Finder → Applications → Utilities → Terminal, or search for Terminal in Spotlight (⌘ Space).


Linux (Ubuntu)
Open the Terminal from your applications menu, or press Ctrl + Alt + T.


Windows
Download and install MobaXterm Home Edition (free). Once installed:

Open MobaXterm
Click Start local terminal

MobaXterm provides a bash shell on Windows and will be used throughout this workshop.

title: 1  Introducing the shell
text:
1.1 What is the shell?
A shell is a program that lets you interact with your computer by typing commands. Instead of clicking on icons and menus — a graphical user interface, or GUI — you type instructions and the computer responds with text output.
The shell we will use is called bash (Bourne Again SHell). It is the default on most Linux systems and on older Macs. On newer Macs the default is zsh, which is nearly identical for our purposes.

title: 1  Introducing the shell
text:
Why bother?
You might wonder why anyone would use a text interface when GUIs exist. Here are three reasons that matter in bioinformatics:

Most bioinformatics tools have no GUI. Programs like BLAST, HMMER, Bowtie, and Samtools are run exclusively from the command line.
Automation. When you need to process hundreds of files, a few lines of shell code replaces hours of pointing and clicking.
Remote computing. High-performance computing clusters — where large genomic analyses actually run — are only accessible via the shell.

title: 1  Introducing the shell
text:
1.2 Opening the terminal
Depending on your operating system, you opened the terminal following the setup instructions in the Preface. You should see a window with some text and a blinking cursor. That text is the prompt.

title: 1  Introducing the shell
text:
1.3 The prompt
The prompt tells you the shell is waiting for input. It typically looks something like this:
username@computer:~$
The exact appearance varies, but what matters is the $ (or % on zsh) at the end — this is the signal that the shell is ready. Do not type the $ when following examples in this workshop. It is shown only to indicate a command you should type.

title: 1  Introducing the shell
text:
1.4 Your first commands
Let’s run a few simple commands to get comfortable.

Identifying yourself (Who am I?)
The command whoami prints your username:
whoami


Checking the time (What time is it?)
The command date prints the current date and time:
date


Clearing the screen (Clean slate)
The command clear clears the terminal screen. Your previous output is not lost — you can still scroll up to see it:
clear






Tip



The keyboard shortcut Ctrl + L does the same thing as clear.




Printing text (Talking to yourself)
The command echo prints whatever you give it back to the screen:
echo "Hello, world"
You will see echo again when we write scripts.

title: 1  Introducing the shell
text:
1.5 The anatomy of a command
Most shell commands follow this pattern:
command [options] [arguments]

command — the programme to run (e.g. ls)
options (or flags) — modify the behaviour, usually starting with - (e.g. -l)
arguments — the target the command acts on (e.g. a filename or directory)

We will see this pattern in action throughout the workshop.







Caution✏️ Exercise 1.1



Run echo with your own name:
echo "Hello, my name is Clara"
Now try running echo without any argument — just type echo and press Enter. What happens?






NoteSolution





echo with no argument prints a blank line. It still runs — it just has nothing to print. This illustrates that commands can often be run without arguments, though the result may not be very useful.











Important🚀 Bonus — for those who want more



Find out what version of bash (or zsh) you are running:
echo $BASH_VERSION
# or on newer Macs:
echo $ZSH_VERSION
The $ before a word tells the shell to treat it as a variable — a named value stored in memory. $BASH_VERSION is a variable the shell sets automatically when it starts.









Tip🔑 Key points




The shell is a text-based interface for interacting with your computer
The prompt ($) signals the shell is ready for input
Commands follow the pattern: command [options] [arguments]
whoami, date, clear, and echo are simple starter commands

title: 2  Navigating the file system
text:
2.1 The file system
Files and directories on a Unix system are arranged in a hierarchy, like an upside-down tree. The base of the tree is called the root directory and is written as /. Every file and directory on the system lives somewhere beneath it.
When you open a terminal, you start in your home directory — typically /home/username on Linux or /Users/username on a Mac. This is your personal space on the system.

title: 2  Navigating the file system
text:
/
├── home/
│   └── username/
│       ├── Documents/
│       └── Downloads/
├── usr/
└── tmp/

title: 2  Navigating the file system
text:
2.2 Where am I? — pwd
The command pwd stands for print working directory. It tells you exactly where you are in the file system:
pwd
Your output will look something like:
/home/username
or on a Mac:
/Users/username

title: 2  Navigating the file system
text:
2.3 What is here? — ls
The command ls lists the contents of your current directory:
ls
You can make the output more informative with flags:



Flag
Effect




ls -F
Adds / after directories, * after executables


ls -l
Long format — shows permissions, size, date


ls -a
Shows hidden files (those starting with .)


ls -lh
Long format with human-readable file sizes









Tip



You can combine flags: ls -lF or ls -lah are both valid.

title: 2  Navigating the file system
text:
2.4 Creating a directory — mkdir
Let’s create a dedicated working directory for this session:
mkdir training
Check it was created:
ls






Note



Directory names should not contain spaces. Use underscores (_) or hyphens (-) instead. For example: my_data or my-data.

title: 2  Navigating the file system
text:
2.5 Moving around — cd
The command cd stands for change directory. Let’s move into the directory we just created:
cd training
Confirm you have moved:
pwd

Going up
Two dots (..) refer to the directory above your current location (the parent directory). To go up one level:
cd ..


Going home
A tilde (~) always refers to your home directory. From anywhere on the system:
cd ~
or simply:
cd
Both take you straight home.

title: 2  Navigating the file system
text:
2.6 Absolute vs. relative paths
A path is the address of a file or directory in the file system.

An absolute path starts from the root (/) and works from anywhere on the system:
cd /home/username/training
A relative path is given relative to where you currently are:
cd training







Tip



Use absolute paths in scripts — they always work regardless of where the script is called from. Use relative paths for quick interactive navigation.

title: 2  Navigating the file system
text:
2.7 Tab completion
Typing long directory or file names is error-prone. Instead, type the first few characters and press Tab — the shell will autocomplete:
cd trai<Tab>
becomes:
cd training
If there are multiple matches, press Tab twice to see all options.






Tip



Tab completion is one of the most valuable habits you can build. Use it constantly.









Caution✏️ Exercise 2.1




Navigate into your training directory
Use pwd to confirm where you are
Go back to your home directory using ..
Go back to training using ~ notation (i.e. cd ~/training)







NoteSolution





cd training
pwd
cd ..
cd ~/training











Caution✏️ Exercise 2.2



Inside training, create two new directories called data and results:
mkdir data results
List the contents to confirm both were created. Then use ls -F — what does the trailing / tell you?






NoteSolution





ls
ls -F
The trailing / indicates that data and results are directories, not files.











Important🚀 Bonus — for those who want more



The tree command prints a visual representation of the directory structure. Try:
tree ~/training
If tree is not installed, you can install it with sudo apt install tree (Ubuntu) or brew install tree (Mac with Homebrew). On MobaXterm it is usually available by default.









Tip🔑 Key points




The Unix file system is a hierarchy rooted at /
pwd prints your current location; ls lists directory contents; cd moves you around
.. refers to the parent directory; ~ refers to your home directory
Tab completion reduces errors and saves time

title: 3  Working with files
text:
3.1 Touching (Creating an empty file)
The command touch creates an empty file — or updates the timestamp of an existing file without changing its contents:

title: 3  Working with files
text:
cd ~/training
touch notes.txt
ls






Note



touch is also used to update the timestamp of an existing file without changing its contents.

title: 3  Working with files
text:
3.2 Copying files — cp
cp copies a file. The syntax is:
cp source destination
For example:
cp notes.txt notes_backup.txt
ls
To copy a whole directory (and everything inside it), use the -r flag (recursive):
cp -r data data_backup

title: 3  Working with files
text:
3.3 Moving and renaming — mv
mv moves a file — but it also serves as the way to rename files in Unix:
# rename
mv notes.txt readme.txt

# move to a subdirectory
mv readme.txt data/readme.txt






Warning



mv will silently overwrite the destination if it already exists. Double-check before moving.

title: 3  Working with files
text:
3.4 Deleting files — rm
rm removes files permanently. There is no recycle bin on the Unix command line.
rm notes_backup.txt
To remove a directory and all its contents:
rm -r data_backup






Warning



Be careful with rm -r. There is no undo. A common safety habit is to use rm -ri, which asks for confirmation before deleting each file.

title: 3  Working with files
text:
3.5 Viewing file contents

cat — print the whole file
cat data/readme.txt
cat prints the entire file to the terminal at once. This is fine for small files, but unwieldy for large ones.


head — first lines
head shows the first 10 lines by default:
head data/readme.txt
Use -n to specify a different number:
head -n 5 data/readme.txt


tail — last lines
tail shows the last 10 lines:
tail data/readme.txt


less — scroll through a file
For large files, less is the tool to use. It opens the file in a scrollable viewer:
less data/readme.txt



Key
Action




Space
Page down


b
Page up


↓ / ↑
Line by line


/pattern
Search for pattern


n
Next search result


q
Quit










Caution✏️ Exercise 3.1




Navigate to ~/training
Create a file called experiment.txt inside the data/ directory
Make a copy of it called experiment_v2.txt in the same directory
Rename experiment.txt to experiment_v1.txt
List the contents of data/ to confirm







NoteSolution





cd ~/training
touch data/experiment.txt
cp data/experiment.txt data/experiment_v2.txt
mv data/experiment.txt data/experiment_v1.txt
ls data/











Important🚀 Bonus — for those who want more



The wc command counts words, lines and characters in a file:
wc data/readme.txt
Use wc -l to count only lines. We will use this in Chapter 5 to count sequences in a FASTA file.









Tip🔑 Key points




touch creates an empty file; cp copies; mv moves or renames; rm deletes permanently
cat prints a whole file; head and tail show the beginning and end; less lets you scroll
Always double-check before using rm — there is no undo

title: 4  Fetching sequence data
text:
4.1 The organism: Pelagibacter ubique HTCC1062
We are going to work with the genome of Pelagibacter ubique strain HTCC1062 — arguably the most ecologically important bacterium on the planet.
Pelagibacter ubique belongs to the SAR11 clade, a group of free-living marine alphaproteobacteria that may account for 25–50% of all microbial cells in the ocean’s surface waters. Its genome (accession NC_007205.1) is small (~1.3 Mb), streamlined, and extraordinarily well-studied.

title: 4  Fetching sequence data
text:
Note



Genome streamlining is an evolutionary strategy in nutrient-poor environments: P. ubique has one of the smallest genomes of any free-living organism, with almost no non-coding DNA and very few regulatory elements.

title: 4  Fetching sequence data
text:
4.2 The FASTA format
A FASTA file is one of the simplest and most widely used formats in bioinformatics. Each sequence entry has two parts:

A header line starting with >, containing the sequence identifier and optional description
One or more lines of sequence data

Example:
>NC_007205.1 Candidatus Pelagibacter ubique HTCC1062, complete sequence
AGTTTTCGAATTTGAATTTTAAGAAGTTTCGAATTTGAATTTGAAGAAATTTCGAATTT
GAATTTGAAAAAGTTTCGAATTTGAATTTGAAGAAATTTCGAATTTGAATTTGAAAAAG
...
FASTA files can contain a single sequence (as here) or many sequences (a multi-FASTA file), which is common for collections of genes or proteins.

title: 4  Fetching sequence data
text:
4.3 Method 1 — efetch (NCBI Entrez Direct)
If you installed Entrez Direct during setup, you can fetch the genome directly from NCBI.
First, move into your data directory:
cd ~/training/data
Then fetch the genome:
efetch -db nucleotide -id NC_007205.1 -format fasta > pelagibacter_ubique.fasta
Breaking this command down:



Part
Meaning




efetch
The NCBI fetch tool


-db nucleotide
Search the nucleotide database


-id NC_007205.1
The accession number we want


-format fasta
Return the sequence in FASTA format


> pelagibacter_ubique.fasta
Redirect the output into a file









Note



The > symbol redirects the output of a command into a file instead of printing it to the screen. We will explore redirection more in Chapter 5.

title: 4  Fetching sequence data
text:
4.4 Method 2 — curl (backup)
If efetch is not available (e.g. on Windows with MobaXterm, or if the installation did not succeed), use curl to download the file directly:
cd ~/training/data
curl -L -o pelagibacter_ubique.fasta \
  https://raw.githubusercontent.com/clarajegousse/training-data/main/pelagibacter_ubique.fasta



Flag
Meaning




-L
Follow redirects


-o pelagibacter_ubique.fasta
Save output to this filename









Tip



wget is an alternative to curl and works the same way for simple downloads:
wget -O pelagibacter_ubique.fasta \
  https://raw.githubusercontent.com/clarajegousse/training-data/main/pelagibacter_ubique.fasta
wget is available on most Linux systems and in MobaXterm. On a Mac, curl is the default.

title: 4  Fetching sequence data
text:
4.5 Verifying the download
Always check that your file downloaded correctly:
ls -lh pelagibacter_ubique.fasta
You should see a file of around 1.4 MB. If the file is 0 bytes or a few hundred bytes, something went wrong.
Take a quick look at the beginning of the file:
head pelagibacter_ubique.fasta
You should see a header line starting with >NC_007205.1 followed by lines of sequence.







Caution✏️ Exercise 4.1




Download the genome using either efetch or curl (whichever works on your system)
Confirm the file size with ls -lh
Use head to view the first 5 lines
Use tail to view the last 5 lines — what do you notice about the end of the sequence?







NoteSolution





# Download (choose one method)
efetch -db nucleotide -id NC_007205.1 -format fasta > pelagibacter_ubique.fasta
# or
curl -L -o pelagibacter_ubique.fasta \
  https://raw.githubusercontent.com/clarajegousse/training-data/main/pelagibacter_ubique.fasta

# Check size
ls -lh pelagibacter_ubique.fasta

# Inspect
head -n 5 pelagibacter_ubique.fasta
tail -n 5 pelagibacter_ubique.fasta
The last few lines are just sequence — the FASTA format has no end marker. The genome sequence continues right to the last line of the file.











Important🚀 Bonus — for those who want more



Try fetching the genome in a different format. GenBank format includes annotations (gene positions, product names, etc.):
efetch -db nucleotide -id NC_007205.1 -format gb > pelagibacter_ubique.gb
head -n 30 pelagibacter_ubique.gb
How does this compare to the FASTA file? What additional information does it contain?









Tip🔑 Key points




FASTA is a simple two-part format: a > header line followed by sequence data
efetch retrieves sequences from NCBI databases directly from the command line
curl (or wget) downloads files from URLs — useful as a fallback
Always verify downloads with ls -lh and head

title: 5  Exploring sequence data
text:
5.1 Searching inside files — grep
grep (global regular expression print) searches a file line by line and prints every line that contains a given pattern.
Let’s confirm our FASTA file has a header:
This prints every line containing >. In a single-sequence FASTA file, there is only one such line.

title: 5  Exploring sequence data
text:
grep ">" pelagibacter_ubique.fasta


Useful grep flags



Flag
Effect




-c
Count matching lines instead of printing them


-i
Case-insensitive matching


-v
Invert — print lines that do not match


-n
Show line numbers



How many header lines are in the file?
grep -c ">" pelagibacter_ubique.fasta
For a single genome sequence the answer is 1. In a multi-FASTA file (e.g. all the genes of an organism), this tells you the number of sequences.

title: 5  Exploring sequence data
text:
5.2 Counting with wc
wc stands for word count but it counts lines, words and characters:
wc pelagibacter_ubique.fasta
Output format: lines  words  characters  filename
To count only lines:
wc -l pelagibacter_ubique.fasta






Note



Each line of sequence in a FASTA file is typically 60 or 70 characters long. If you subtract 1 (the header line) and multiply the remaining line count by the line length, you get an estimate of genome size — though the last line is usually shorter.

title: 5  Exploring sequence data
text:
5.3 Chaining commands with pipes — |
A pipe (|) takes the output of one command and passes it directly as input to the next, without creating an intermediate file.
Let’s count the lines in the output of grep without saving to a file:
grep -v ">" pelagibacter_ubique.fasta | wc -l
This: 1. grep -v ">" — selects all lines that are not headers (i.e. just sequence lines) 2. | wc -l — counts those lines
You can chain as many commands as you need:
grep -v ">" pelagibacter_ubique.fasta | wc -c
This counts the total number of characters in the sequence lines — a rough proxy for genome length in base pairs (note: it includes newline characters, so the true bp count is slightly lower).

title: 5  Exploring sequence data
text:
5.4 Redirecting output — >
You have already used > to save efetch output to a file. You can redirect the output of any command:
grep ">" pelagibacter_ubique.fasta > headers.txt
cat headers.txt
Use >> to append to an existing file rather than overwriting it:
echo "Downloaded from NCBI: NC_007205.1" >> headers.txt
cat headers.txt







Caution✏️ Exercise 5.1




How many sequence lines (non-header lines) are in pelagibacter_ubique.fasta?
Use a pipe to count the total number of characters in the sequence (excluding the header). How does this compare to the expected genome size of ~1.3 Mb?







NoteSolution





# Count sequence lines
grep -v ">" pelagibacter_ubique.fasta | wc -l

# Count sequence characters
grep -v ">" pelagibacter_ubique.fasta | wc -c
The character count will be slightly higher than 1,308,759 bp (the true genome size) because wc -c also counts the newline character (\n) at the end of each line. The number of sequence lines multiplied by ~60 gives a good estimate.











Important🚀 Bonus — for those who want more



Search for the string ATG (the standard start codon) in the sequence:
grep -o "ATG" pelagibacter_ubique.fasta | wc -l
The -o flag prints only the matched part of each line (one match per line), so wc -l counts the total occurrences. How many start codons can you find?
Then try searching for a specific motif — for example the sigma-70 promoter consensus TATAAT:
grep -c "TATAAT" pelagibacter_ubique.fasta






NoteNote





This is a very naive search — it finds the string in any reading frame and on either strand. Real promoter prediction uses more sophisticated tools. But it shows the power of even simple shell commands for biological questions.












Tip🔑 Key points




grep searches for patterns in files; -c counts matches; -v inverts the search
wc -l counts lines; wc -c counts characters
The pipe | passes output from one command directly into the next — no intermediate file needed
> redirects output to a file (overwrites); >> appends to an existing file

title: 6  Conclusion
text:
6.1 What we covered
In this session you have gone from opening a terminal for the first time to downloading and querying a real bacterial genome. Specifically, you can now:
These are the building blocks of almost everything done in command-line bioinformatics.

title: 6  Conclusion
text:
Navigate a Unix file system using pwd, ls, and cd
Create, copy, move and delete files and directories
Download sequences from NCBI using efetch or curl
Inspect biological files with head, tail, and less
Search for patterns with grep and count with wc
Chain commands together with the pipe |

title: 6  Conclusion
text:
6.2 Command reference



Command
What it does




pwd
Print working directory


ls
List directory contents


cd
Change directory


mkdir
Make a new directory


touch
Create an empty file


cp
Copy a file or directory


mv
Move or rename a file


rm
Delete a file (-r for directories)


cat
Print file contents


head
Show first lines of a file


tail
Show last lines of a file


less
Scroll through a file


wc
Count lines, words, characters


grep
Search for patterns in files


echo
Print text to the terminal


man
Open the manual for a command


efetch
Fetch records from NCBI databases


curl
Download files from URLs

title: 6  Conclusion
text:
6.3 Where to go next
The shell is deep. Here are some natural next steps depending on your goals:
If you want to go further with genomics:

Data Carpentry: Shell Genomics — redirection, loops, scripts
Software Carpentry: The Unix Shell — comprehensive foundations

If you want to write scripts:

Start with simple bash scripts: a file ending in .sh containing the commands you would type interactively
Learn about for loops, variables, and conditionals

If you need to work on a remote server (HPC):

ssh username@server.address — connect to a remote machine
scp or rsync — copy files to/from a remote machine
SLURM or PBS — job schedulers common on university HPC clusters

title: 6  Conclusion
text:
6.4 Acknowledgements
This workshop is adapted from:

Data Carpentry: Introduction to the shell for genomics data (Becker et al. 2019)
Clara Jégousse’s previous workshops: intro-shell-cmd-line and tsl-shell-genomics

title: 1  Introducing the shell
text:
1.3 The prompt (Am I ready?)
The prompt tells you the shell is waiting for input. It typically looks something like this:
username@computer:~$
The exact appearance varies, but what matters is the $ (or % on zsh) at the end — this signals that the shell is ready. Do not type the $ when following examples in this workshop. It is shown only to indicate a command you should type.

title: 2  Navigating the file system
text:
2.2 Print working directory (Where am I?)
At any moment, you are working inside one directory — your current working directory. The command pwd (print working directory) tells you exactly where that is:
pwd
Your output will look something like:
/home/username
or on a Mac:
/Users/username

title: 2  Navigating the file system
text:
2.3 Listing (What is there?)
The command ls (list) shows the contents of your current directory:
ls
On its own, ls gives you a simple list of names. Flags make it more informative:



Command
What it shows




ls -F
Adds / after directories, * after executables


ls -l
Long format — permissions, size, date


ls -a
All files, including hidden ones (starting with .)


ls -lh
Long format with human-readable file sizes









Tip



Flags can be combined: ls -lF and ls -lah are both valid. The order of flags generally does not matter.

title: 2  Navigating the file system
section: 2.4 Making a directory (Creating a place to work)
text:
2.4 Making a directory (Creating a place to work)
The command mkdir (make directory) creates a new directory:
mkdir training
Check it was created:
ls






Note



Directory names should not contain spaces. Use underscores (_) or hyphens (-) instead — for example, my_data or shell-intro. Spaces in filenames cause all sorts of problems on the command line.

title: 2  Navigating the file system
text:
2.5 Changing directory (Going somewhere)
The command cd (change directory) moves you into a different directory. Let’s go into the one we just created:
cd training
Confirm you have moved:
pwd

Going up (Back to the parent)
Two dots (..) always refer to the directory above your current location — the parent directory:
cd ..


Going home
A tilde (~) always refers to your home directory. From anywhere on the system:
cd ~
or simply:
cd
Both take you straight home.

title: 2  Navigating the file system
section: 2.7 Tab completion (Let the shell do the typing)
text:
2.7 Tab completion (Let the shell do the typing)
Typing long directory or file names is error-prone. Type the first few characters and press Tab — the shell will autocomplete:
cd trai<Tab>
becomes:
cd training
If there are multiple matches, press Tab twice to see all options.






Tip



Tab completion is one of the most valuable habits you can build at the command line. Use it constantly — it saves time and prevents typos.

title: 2  Navigating the file system
section: 2.8 Reading the manual (How does this work?)
text:
2.8 Reading the manual (How does this work?)
Every command comes with a built-in manual. The command man (manual) opens it:
man ls
Use the arrow keys or Space to scroll. Press q to quit.






Tip



No one memorises all the options for every command. The manual is always there. For a quicker summary, many commands also accept --help:
ls --help









Caution✏️ Exercise 2.1




Navigate into your training directory
Use print working directory to confirm where you are
Go back to your home directory using ..
Go back to training using the ~ notation







NoteSolution





cd training
pwd
cd ..
cd ~/training











Caution✏️ Exercise 2.2



Inside training, create two new directories called data and results in a single command:
mkdir data results
List the contents with ls -F. What does the trailing / tell you?






NoteSolution





ls -F
The trailing / indicates that data and results are directories, not files. Files have no trailing character; executable files get a *.











Important🚀 Bonus — for those who want more



The command tree prints a visual representation of a directory structure:
tree ~/training
If tree is not installed: sudo apt install tree (Ubuntu), brew install tree (Mac with Homebrew), or it may already be available in MobaXterm.
Also try: what happens when you run cd with no argument at all? Where do you end up?









Tip🔑 Key points




The Unix file system is a hierarchy rooted at /
pwd tells you where you are; ls shows what is there; cd moves you around; mkdir creates directories
.. refers to the parent directory; ~ refers to your home directory
Tab completion reduces errors and saves time — use it constantly
man opens the manual for any command

title: 3  Working with files
text:
cd ~/training
touch notes.txt
ls






Note



Empty files might seem useless, but touch is handy for quickly creating placeholder files, or for testing commands before applying them to real data.

title: 3  Working with files
text:
3.2 Copying (Making a duplicate)
The command cp (copy) duplicates a file. The syntax is:
cp source destination
For example:
cp notes.txt notes_backup.txt
ls
To copy a whole directory and everything inside it, add the -r flag (recursive):
cp -r data data_backup

title: 3  Working with files
text:
3.3 Moving (Relocating or renaming)
The command mv (move) moves a file to a new location — but it also serves as the way to rename files in Unix, since there is no separate rename command:
# rename a file
mv notes.txt readme.txt

# move it into a subdirectory
mv readme.txt data/readme.txt






Warning



mv will silently overwrite the destination if a file with that name already exists there. Always double-check before moving.

title: 3  Working with files
text:
3.4 Removing (Deleting permanently)
The command rm (remove) deletes files. Unlike moving something to the Trash, there is no undo:
rm notes_backup.txt
To remove a directory and all its contents:
rm -r data_backup






Warning



Be very careful with rm -r. A common safety habit is to use rm -ri instead, which asks for confirmation before deleting each item:
rm -ri data_backup

title: 3  Working with files
text:
3.5 Concatenating (Printing a whole file)
The command cat (catenate) prints the entire contents of a file to the terminal:
cat data/readme.txt
cat is fast and simple, but prints everything at once — not ideal for large files.

title: 3  Working with files
section: 3.6 Head and tail (Peeking at the beginning or end)
text:
3.6 Head and tail (Peeking at the beginning or end)
head shows the first 10 lines of a file by default:
head data/readme.txt
Use -n to choose a different number of lines:
head -n 5 data/readme.txt
tail shows the last 10 lines:
tail data/readme.txt
These are especially useful for large biological data files — you can check the format without loading the whole thing.

title: 3  Working with files
text:
3.7 Less (Scrolling through a large file)
For files too large to comfortably cat, less opens them in an interactive scrollable viewer:
less data/readme.txt



Key
Action




Space
Page down


b
Page up


↓ / ↑
One line at a time


/pattern
Search for pattern


n
Next search result


q
Quit









Tip



You will use less constantly when working with FASTQ, FASTA, and other large genomics files. It never loads the whole file into memory, so it works even on files that are gigabytes in size.









Caution✏️ Exercise 3.1




Navigate to ~/training
Create a file called experiment.txt inside the data/ directory
Make a copy of it called experiment_v2.txt in the same directory
Rename experiment.txt to experiment_v1.txt
List the contents of data/ to confirm







NoteSolution





cd ~/training
touch data/experiment.txt
cp data/experiment.txt data/experiment_v2.txt
mv data/experiment.txt data/experiment_v1.txt
ls data/











Important🚀 Bonus — for those who want more



The command wc (word count) counts lines, words and characters in a file:
wc data/readme.txt
The output format is: lines  words  characters  filename
Use wc -l to count only lines. We will use this in Chapter 5 to count sequences in a FASTA file.









Tip🔑 Key points




touch creates an empty file; cp copies; mv moves or renames; rm deletes permanently — there is no undo
cat prints a whole file; head and tail show the beginning and end; less lets you scroll through large files
Always double-check before using rm -r
